tweets.text:
Agree with your circles.  Warsh has no cover to cut yet and happy to wait.  Here's my FOMC outlook

My view on the next 4 Fed meetings 

I think the same thing I've thought for awhile

The fed cannot and should not act when supply shocks are the root cause of inflation so shouldn't hike due to tariff or war driven oil inflation

The fed should be highly focused on demand side inflation and wage inflation and I think the balance sheet policy is keeping demand side inflation above target due to wealth effect consumption spending. 

I think in order to bring inflation back to target the Fed needs to adjust its balance sheet policy

In terms of short rate I think we are close enough to neutral as to generate no need for cuts or hikes in any meaningful  amount.

As to the current FOMC stance  half want to do an insurance hike or two and half including the chair are happy to wait to later in the fall to hike if necessary but don't expect it will be necessary.  

Warsh specifically won't advocate for a hike until his task forces work is done and that buys him some time to stay on pause probably through the next three meetings (the midterms). 

If inflation remains high for the next 4 months (June -September data releases) a December hike post midterm will definetly happen and by then a legitimate hiking cycle may actually occur.  

That's my view.  Pause and "task force" through midterms and do what is necessary in December. Which if lucky will be a cut but more likely a hike

* LLMs giving simple explanations & visualizations on demand for anything I don't understand

* Getting prompted in the evenings for daily micro-lessons on Telegram by my Hermes Agent, which keeps track of my learning & lesson plans

The educational renaissance is here. Just not evenly distributed.

tweets.text:
There's always some online debate going on about which AI model is the smartest right now.

Benchmarks, context windows, new model launches - all of it gets measured obsessively.

But the problem that isn't discussed enough is that agents drop the entire context when you close the tab, and you have to start the next session from a blank slate.

For me, it’s a daily annoyance. But at enterprise scale, it becomes an expensive time-waster because teams keep feeding agents the same context over and over.

I believe we'll see a 10x increase in startups focusing on solving this issue in the next 12 months.

The market is still largely untapped compared to other AI primitives. Projects like @WalrusProtocol are working to close this gap, but overall the opportunities in this field remain asymmetric.

I believe that the next meaningful AI unlock is memory that survives the session.

Disclosure: I'm holding some $WAL.

tweets.text:
tldr even if ethereum becomes giga successful -- priority fees will probably not rise and may even go down a lot, and that is a good thing.

if ethereum scales in both tech and adoption, base fees (i.e. the fixed fee paid by the marginal transaction for inclusion in the block) can certainly increase a lot, and there isn't really a ceiling for that. 

1M txns/s paid $0.1 cent each? that's $315b in run rate revenue per year. something like that is probably Ethereum's bull case for me.

but there is simply no such case for prio fees to increase a lot, and one big reason to believe they will actually continue to fall over time.

for a primer, searchers and retail traders pay prio fees  price to access a *specific* piece of state before other transactors. a block can have a base fee of 1 gwei, with someone paying 1m gwei to access the first slot in the block. even though ethereum's adoption and txn activity increased, prio fees have stayed relatively constant at 500-600m/year for the last 4 years. 

many mev experts, including me, have predicted that priority fees would likely decrease even as activity increases a lot, and the reason is that most of these conflicts should never "trickle down" to the validator to begin with. they are like free dollar bills that someone drops on the street, and if they are smart, they will simply stop dropping it. and if they are not smart, someone who is will replace their app over time.

when you zoom in on what conflicts searchers are paying for priority in, its primarily two things: arbitrage and liquidations (which are itself a form of arbitrage of buying an underpriced lending market position and selling it for a profit). 

in both cases, the conflict used to reach the chain even though there sits someone upstream who could resolve this conflict already: e.g. a wallets running backrunning auctions on their user's orderflow (metamask, rabby, everyone), DEXs selling the first right to arbitrage (angstrom), DEXs paying to update their quotes before the first order can hit per block (propAMMs), lending markets selling the right to liquidate a position (chainlink svr + aave) - the list goes on. 

even though I predicted this trend of "apps taking control of their own ordering" for years, 2026 is the year when it finally reached not just wallets but DEXs and lending markets as well, at scale, and Defi is becoming much better for it. 

this has several consequences for ethereum: if you think that Defi is ethereum's main usecase and will be responsible for much of its future growth, this trend is amazing. it will make Defi spreads tighter and fees lower, making it ultimately more competitive against cefi and tradfi markets.

second, prio fees will likely never matter again for stakers, and should not be a big part of the discussion for issuance reduction. another update that comes up occasionally is mev-burn - on the face of it, probably not worth the complexity.

if you take away one thing from the thread, its that "95% of priority fees belong the apps or wallets who create them, and will flow there over time". ethereum has been benefiting from the inefficiency of the apps built on top of it for so much value to trickle down, but this trend is now in full reversal. and a future when apps make trillions of trading and lending volume per day, while paying hardly any priority fees - that future is actually very good, because it means the apps are well designed and competitive with tradfi, in spite of running on a blockchain. 

if Ethereum wins, it will be on the back of a small base fee that millions of actors pay, that can still accumulate to a whole lot of revenue per year.

Think of every decision as a bet with a probability and a reward for being right and a probability and a penalty for being wrong. Normally a winning decision is one with a positive expected value, meaning that the reward times its probability of occurring is greater than the penalty times its probability of occurring, with the best decision being the one with the highest expected value.
#principleoftheday

thread_tweets.text: Gamification is also contradictory with learning something truly complex. Thinking about maths for example, the most efficient way to grasp the solution to a problem is usually to start on a blank sheet and force yourself to try to rewrite it yourself, but that's also an exhausting task, whereas a multiple choice quiz (very gamified, very "easy" to do) won't get you very far

I remember interviewing someone a few years ago and they said: “Base is going to go live and eat Arbitrum whole.”  

Needless to say, they didn’t get the job, but moreso, Arbitrum has held up very well relative to Base and still remains the largest L2 to date…

… Now Robinhood chain is mainnet and will further strengthen Arbitrum position. 

Very easy to lose hope in the bear market. Arbitrum is doing very well and will continue to do so. 🐧

tweets.text:
New blog post focused on a fun paper by @danivilardell77 and @sjbreck

They study voter privacy via bribery games and call it B-Privacy.     

Why?     

Privacy in weighted voting is harder than it looks.  

Even if every ballot is encrypted, the final tally itself can leak how people voted.    

Worse: that leakage can make vote buying enfoceable   

It is not straight forward to make a weighted voting system private without hiding the tally.    

So, can you do something in between, where there is "enough" voting privacy such that the briber's cost is increased by several multiples?     

Find out in the next tweet 👇
